metadata.summary: Four-layer audit (Functional → Reliable → Usable → Delightful) to upgrade from MVP to Minimum Lovable Product. MLP = MVP + targeted delight in high-frequency interactions.

content.title: MLP Planning
content.summary: Plan the upgrade from a validated MVP to a Minimum Lovable Product: a four-layer audit (Functional → Reliable → Usable → Delightful) that identifies the specific gap between "it works" and "I love it". MLP = MVP + targeted delight in high-frequency, high-visibility interactions. The process ends with explicit completion criteria tied to retention and NPS targets, not feature counts.

why.p: MVPs validate ideas but fail to retain users. Competing products in crowded markets are already "good enough", so users try an MVP and leave for better UX. Retention requires emotional connection, and the four-layer model gives a concrete, auditable path to it: delight cannot be bolted on randomly — it must be layered on top of solid function, reliability, and usability.

list.item: MVP is live, core hypothesis validated, but Day-7/30 retention is weak.
Users complete the core flow but don't return or recommend.
You're preparing a second-wave release specifically targeting retention.
NPS is below 30 and you need a structured diagnosis.
Problem hypothesis is still unvalidated — validate with MVP first.
No usage data yet; MLP audit requires actual interaction patterns to rate layers.
B2B/enterprise deal where retention is driven by contract, not experience.
Prioritising more features over polishing existing ones is a strategic choice — revisit after feature bets resolve.

section.name: four-layer-model
section.p: The four-layer model that defines the path from a functional MVP to a Minimum Lovable Product, with a rating scale and MLP threshold rule.

Minimum Lovable Product is the smallest version that delivers core value, creates emotional connection, and gives users a reason to stay and recommend. MLP = MVP + delight. Not more features — better experience.

rule.statement: Every layer must be solid before adding the next. Delight built on unreliable foundations is wasted.
rule.rationale: Users notice inconsistency before they notice charm. A delightful animation on a crashy flow increases frustration, not affection.

rule.statement: Layer 1 — Functional: core job completes, no blocking bugs, acceptable performance.
rule.rationale: MVP baseline. Without this, nothing else matters.

rule.statement: Layer 2 — Reliable: consistent experience, error handling, edge cases covered, works as expected every time.
rule.rationale: Reliability is the prerequisite for trust. Users forgive a rough UI; they do not forgive data loss or silent failures.

rule.statement: Layer 3 — Usable: easy to learn, efficient to use, minimal friction, good defaults.
rule.rationale: Usability reduces the cognitive tax per session. High friction kills retention even when the product works perfectly.

rule.statement: Layer 4 — Delightful: emotional satisfaction, surprising moments, premium feel, shareable moments.
rule.rationale: Delight creates word-of-mouth and emotional attachment that purely functional products cannot generate.

rule.statement: Rate each core feature 1–5 on each layer. MLP threshold: all features at 4+ on all four layers.
rule.rationale: The threshold is not perfection (5) but sufficiency (4). Rating below 4 on any layer signals a gap that blocks MLP readiness.

rule.statement: Polish priority = user pain × frequency × visibility. Focus on high-frequency, high-visibility interactions first.
rule.rationale: Delight in rarely-seen flows is invisible. The same effort on the core daily action multiplies impact across all sessions.

section.p: MVP goal is to validate; MLP goal is to retain. MVP focus is functionality; MLP focus is experience. MVP emotion is "it works"; MLP emotion is "I love it". Word-of-mouth is unlikely from MVP users and likely from MLP users.

section.name: mlp-planning-process
section.p: Five-step process for planning the gap between a validated MVP and a Minimum Lovable Product.

rule.statement: Step 1: Audit Your MVP. For each core feature, rate 1–5 on all four layers. Any feature below 4 on any layer is a gap that must be closed before declaring MLP.
rule.rationale: The audit makes the gap visible and concrete, replacing "the UX needs work" with specific layer deficits per feature.

rule.statement: Step 2: Identify Delight Opportunities. Scan four moments for delight: first impression (onboarding), core action (the main thing users do), success moments (when they win), and recovery moments (when things go wrong).
rule.rationale: Delight in error states is disproportionately memorable — it converts frustration into loyalty. Most products ignore this.

list.item: Speed delight: faster than expected (instant search results)
Simplicity delight: easier than expected (one-click checkout)
Anticipation delight: knows what they need (smart defaults)
Celebration delight: acknowledges success (confetti on completion)

rule.statement: Step 3: Prioritize Polish. Apply the formula: polish priority = user pain × frequency × visibility. Only invest in high-frequency, high-visibility interactions.
rule.rationale: Polish on a rarely-seen screen is invisible to most users. The same effort on the daily core action compounds across every session.

rule.statement: Step 4: Define MLP Criteria. Write explicit MLP criteria for your target user segment covering all four layers with measurable outcomes. Template: "For [user segment] MLP means: [functional criteria], [reliable criteria], [usable criteria], [delightful criteria]. Measured by: [verification method]."
rule.rationale: Without explicit criteria, MLP becomes subjective and the team will never agree it has been reached.

rule.statement: Step 5: Plan the Gap. For each feature, document: current state, MLP target, what is missing, and effort. This becomes the MLP backlog.
rule.rationale: The gap analysis converts the audit rating into actionable tasks with known scope.

section.name: mlp-planning-examples
section.p: Worked examples of MVP-to-MLP upgrade paths with layer-by-layer improvements and measured outcomes.

example.title: Note-Taking App
example.note: MVP: create, edit, delete notes, basic formatting — functional but bland. NPS 30, Day-30 retention weak.

MLP additions by layer — Reliable: auto-save with visual confirmation. Usable: keyboard shortcuts, quick capture. Delight: smooth animations, satisfying micro-sounds, Zen mode for focused writing, clever empty states.
Result: retention increased 40%, NPS rose from 30 to 55.

example.title: Invoicing Tool
example.note: MVP: create invoices, send to clients — functional but clunky.

MLP additions by layer — Reliable: payment confirmation notifications. Usable: templates from past invoices, client auto-fill. Delight: celebration on payment received, "You've earned $X this month" dashboard.
Result: users create 2x more invoices, NPS 60.

antipattern.description: Adding more features instead of polishing existing ones when retention is low.
antipattern.reason: More features increase scope without addressing the quality gap. Users who left due to rough UX won't return for more features with the same rough UX.

antipattern.description: Skipping MVP validation and going straight to MLP polish.
antipattern.reason: Polish on an unvalidated core value is expensive waste. Validate the job-to-be-done first, then polish the delivery.

antipattern.description: Surface-level polish (color changes, fonts) without addressing interaction quality.
antipattern.reason: Cosmetic polish without fixing interaction friction does not move retention or NPS.

antipattern.description: Generic delight not tailored to the specific audience.
antipattern.reason: Confetti on task completion delights consumer app users; it annoys enterprise finance users in the same moment. Delight must match emotional context.

antipattern.description: Adding Layer 4 delight without completing Layers 1–3.
antipattern.reason: Delight on top of unreliable or unusable flows increases frustration. Users notice the inconsistency more than the charm.

template.purpose: Full MLP planning document: feature audit table, delight opportunities, polish backlog, completion criteria, measurement plan.
Focused sprint template for adding delight to one workflow area.
